dek: Hamilton County is well-off, but not the richest place in America — and it still turned more of its private money into shared, public wealth than almost anywhere in the pilot. That is the whole point.

sections.heading: The roundabout tell
body: Drive through Carmel and you almost never stop. The city built more than 150 roundabouts and kept only about a dozen signalized intersections — a deliberate, decades-long choice that most American suburbs never made. Blended across Hamilton County's four cities, the roundabout ratio lands at 0.42, far above the 0.30 we treat as strong; in Carmel alone it approaches 0.9.

This is not a design fetish. Roundabouts are cheaper to run because there are no signals to power or maintain, they cut the severity of crashes because cars slow instead of racing a yellow, and they keep traffic moving even in a blackout. Hamilton County's roads carry it out: about 7% of pavement rates poor, roughly 3% of bridges, and traffic deaths near 7 per 100,000. Under it all is a real network for people not in cars — the Monon Trail and an extensive multi-use path system, with sidewalk-and-path coverage that field survey confirms as unusually complete. Movement is one of the county's strongest pillars, and it earned it.

sections.heading: Built for people we will never know
body: The public realm here is not an amenity bolted onto private prosperity; it is the county's civic signature. The Palladium and the Center for the Performing Arts anchor the Carmel Arts & Design District. Midtown and the Monon Community Center give people a reason to be outside their houses. Playgrounds score 90 out of 100 on field inspection — modern equipment, safety surfacing, real upkeep — and library use runs about 4.5 visits per resident, well above the national median near 3.3, with Carmel Clay and Hamilton East both ranking among Indiana's top systems.

Longtime mayor Jim Brainard framed the reasoning plainly: build "for our children and grandchildren and people we will never know." That is what a walkable town center scoring 92, an investment-trend score of 95, and roughly $900 per resident in annual capital spending actually buy. And it is not Carmel alone — Fishers is building out its Nickel Plate District, and Westfield's Grand Park adds more than 400 acres of public ground. Public Realm, Civic Fabric, and Stewardship all rate near the top of the pilot.

sections.heading: Rich, but that isn't the story
body: Yes, the private numbers are high. Median household income is about $125,500 — roughly 50% above the U.S. figure and the highest of any Indiana county — and 62.7% of adults hold a bachelor's degree, nearly double the national rate. Life expectancy of 81 years leads the state, and only about 4.5% of the county's children live in poverty.

But plenty of American counties are this rich or richer, and quietly let their commons thin out — cash out the wealth and skip the shared part. Hamilton County did the opposite. Its Sovereignty Gap sits around -10, meaning it built more common wealth than its incomes alone would predict. It converted private money into things everyone can use, which is a different and rarer accomplishment than simply being wealthy.

sections.heading: The exemplar
body: A Civic Wealth Index near 79.6 places Hamilton County second in the pilot, and it gets there the honest way — strong nearly across the board rather than carried by one showcase project. The point it proves is not that money makes a good place. It is that a place can choose to turn its money into public goods, and that the choice shows up, measurably, in the roads, the trails, the parks, and the halls where people who never met still share the same town.
